quiz.title: Unit 11 Quiz 8, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Brønsted-Lowry Theory and Conjugate Pairs
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Brønsted-Lowry Theory, Conjugate Acid-Base Pairs, Amphoteric Substances, Proton Transfer Reactions

questions.question: Which is a conjugate acid–base pair?
questions.explanation: HCl and Cl⁻ differ by one proton (H⁺). When HCl donates a proton, it becomes Cl⁻, making them a conjugate acid-base pair.

questions.question: Which substance can act as both an acid and a base?
questions.explanation: Water (H₂O) is amphoteric — it can act as both an acid (donating a proton to become OH⁻) and a base (accepting a proton to become H₃O⁺).

questions.question: A substance that can act as both an acid and a base is called:
questions.explanation: An amphoteric substance can act as both an acid and a base. Water is the most common example, but others include Al(OH)₃ and many metal hydroxides.

questions.question: Which of the following does not form an acidic salt?
questions.explanation: Hydrochloric acid (HCl) is a monoprotic strong acid. It does not form acidic salts because it has only one ionizable proton. Phosphoric acid (triprotic) forms salts like NaH₂PO₄ and Na₂HPO₄, carbonic acid (diprotic) forms NaHCO₃, and sulfuric acid (diprotic) forms NaHSO₄ — all are acidic salts.

questions.question: The chemical nature of calcium phosphate, which is the main component of tooth enamel, is:
questions.explanation: Calcium phosphate is amphoteric, meaning it can react with both acids and bases. This property is important for tooth enamel as it allows it to resist demineralization by acids and remineralization in the presence of saliva.

questions.question: In the reaction NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, what is the Brønsted–Lowry acid?
questions.explanation: H₂O donates a proton to NH₃, forming OH⁻ and NH₄⁺. Since H₂O donates a proton, it acts as the Brønsted-Lowry acid.

questions.question: In the reaction NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, what is the conjugate acid?
questions.explanation: NH₃ accepts a proton to become NH₄⁺. Therefore, NH₄⁺ is the conjugate acid of the base NH₃.

questions.question: Which species is the strongest Brønsted–Lowry acid?
questions.explanation: H₃O⁺ (hydronium ion) is the strongest Brønsted-Lowry acid among these options. It readily donates protons to bases. NH₃ is actually a base, OH⁻ is a strong base, and H₂O is neutral with weak acidic properties.

questions.question: Which theory explains acid–base reactions in non-aqueous solutions?
questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry theory defines acids as proton donors and bases as proton acceptors, which applies to all solvents and even gas-phase reactions, unlike Arrhenius theory which is limited to aqueous solutions.

questions.question: In the reaction H₂SO₄ + H₂O → H₃O⁺ + HSO₄⁻, what is the conjugate base?
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ donates a proton to become HSO₄⁻. Therefore, HSO₄⁻ is the conjugate base of the acid H₂SO₄.

questions.question: What happens to an acid after it donates a proton? It becomes a/its _____________
questions.explanation: When an acid donates a proton, it becomes its conjugate base. For example, HCl (acid) donates a proton to become Cl⁻ (conjugate base).

questions.question: Which pair represents a conjugate acid–base pair?
questions.explanation: NH₄⁺ and NH₃ differ by one proton. NH₄⁺ can donate a proton to become NH₃, making them a conjugate acid-base pair.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered Brønsted-Lowry theory and conjugate pairs! 🧪🔬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review identifying conjugate acids and bases.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on proton transfer in acid-base reactions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review Brønsted-Lowry theory, conjugate pairs, and amphoteric substances.
